name: Doherty Threshold: Keep System Response Under 400ms
trigger: An interaction depends on system response time, a feature performs network or compute work in the user's path, or perceived performance is being designed.
recommendation: Aim to keep system response under about 400ms so the user and system operate at pace and engagement stays high. Where true speed is constrained, engineer perceived performance: optimistic UI, skeleton screens, instant local feedback, prefetching likely next actions, and progressive rendering. Faster, sub-400ms loops keep users in flow and increase productivity.
rejection: Reject blocking the user for seconds with no perceived-performance technique, full-page reloads where partial updates suffice, and 'we'll just add a spinner' as a substitute for addressing avoidable latency on hot paths.
validation: Measure interaction-to-response latency on key paths and target under 400ms
Where backend latency is unavoidable, verify optimistic UI, skeletons, or prefetch are used
evidence: Doherty & Thadani (1982), IBM: productivity rises sharply when system response drops below 400ms
Reinforced by Core Web Vitals INP and NN/g response-time guidance
